---
title: "Wake Up Babe, New Quarto Product Just Dropped"
description: "A first look at Quarto's new `great-docs` package, why I care, and why I think it might finally give Python package docs a gentler on-ramp."
author: "Tinashe M. Tapera"
date: "2026-04-15"
image: "FB6D95E2-9380-4DBB-856D-3D92D9BBC6A6.jpeg"
categories: ["writing", "python", "documentation", "quarto", "great-docs"]
title-block-banner: true
filters:
  - lightbox
lightbox: auto
---

Posit just announced the release of their new Quarto-based Python package documentation site generator: [`great-docs`](https://posit-dev.github.io/great-docs/). If you want the source too, the GitHub repo is already live [here](https://github.com/posit-dev/great-docs).

::: {.callout-note}
This is part 1 of a now much saner three-part series.

Part 2 is the deeper walk-through of `great-docs` itself. Part 3 is the notebook-driven development workflow I actually care most about.
:::

If you want the 30-second version, the docs say `great-docs` is a Python package documentation site generator that:

- inspects your package and auto-generates API reference pages
- can document Click-based CLIs
- turns your `README.md` into a landing page
- renders narrative docs from a `user_guide/` directory
- supports extra sections for tutorials, recipes, blog posts, and similar content
- builds the final site with Quarto

The happy path is pretty small:

```bash
pip install great-docs

great-docs init
great-docs build
great-docs preview
```

The docs also note two important practical constraints up front: it wants Python `>=3.11`, and Quarto is the rendering engine doing the heavy lifting under the hood.

In the R world, we’ve been blessed for many years with [`pkgdown`](https://pkgdown.r-lib.org/), and I’ll be honest: it is one of my top reasons to start a multilingual project in R instead of Python. With `pkgdown`, you use [Roxygen tags](https://roxygen2.r-lib.org/reference/tags-rd.html) to annotate your functions, and from there most of the annoying package documentation work becomes civilized. Today, `pkgdown` sites are ubiquitous and instantly identifiable, and in my opinion they are a strong indicator of code quality and attention to detail.

Python, of course, has had documentation tooling for a long time too. The first time I documented a Python package, I used [Sphinx](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/), and it was...alright. It was certainly more work than I wanted, and it felt like I had to stop developing to go become a documentation engineer for a while. Since then, Python has accumulated multiple docstring conventions and documentation workflows, including [Google Style](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html), [NumPy style](https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html), and Sphinx-style docstrings. One of my long-running gripes with Python is that because it is so easy to publish new things, the ecosystem can become a bit of an "anything goes" frontier, and the documentation space is no different.

Each time somebody tries to establish the one true standard, we end up back in the old "conflicting standards" comic:

![XKCD conflicting standards comic](https://imgs.xkcd.com/comics/standards.png)

Now we have `great-docs`. So why do I think this one might actually matter?

## `great-docs` is NOT a Docstring Style Specification

This was the first thing I wanted to verify in the docs, because if Posit had tried to invent yet another docstring religion, I would have been far less interested.

Thankfully, that is not what is happening here.

`great-docs` is a Quarto-driven documentation generator, not a new docstring style. According to the docs and repo, it detects and works with NumPy, Google, and Sphinx-style docstrings. That is exactly the right move. Instead of asking the Python community to start yet another standards war, it just accepts the reality that the war already happened and tries to produce one clean output from the mess.

That alone makes it instantly more plausible to me.

## `great-docs` is Driven By Quarto

Quarto is clearly picking up speed in the data science community, and I care a lot about tools that meet scientists and research programmers where they already work.

![Google Search Trends for Quarto+Posit](quarto-search-trend.png)

The easier it is to integrate Quarto into existing software development infrastructure, the more confidence I have that a tool will actually stick. Quarto already gives us documents, websites, blogs, books, notebooks, and an extension system that is much more fun than it has any right to be.[^1]

If `great-docs` had shown up with its own peculiar rendering stack, I would have been skeptical. Showing up as "use Quarto, but use it for package docs too" makes much more sense.

[^1]: The extension ecosystem is already getting interesting. The [`awesome-quarto`](https://github.com/mcanouil/awesome-quarto) repo is a good place to poke around.

## Pseudo-Corporate Backing

Hear me out.

As an academic and a FOSS-minded person, I am usually wary any time a company starts drifting toward "this should be the standard." But in this case we are talking about Posit, which has already earned a lot of trust from people who build analytical software for a living. Quarto is one of their flagship projects, and `great-docs` feels like a natural extension of that ecosystem rather than an opportunistic side quest.

If you have already trusted Posit with Quarto, I do not think it is a stretch to trust them to take a serious swing at package documentation too.

## Multilinguists Will Now Have An Easy On-Ramp

There are a lot of R users who still feel a bit skittish about Python. Part of that is cultural. R tends to feel a little more analyst-native, while Python often feels like you are stepping into the software engineers' world whether you meant to or not.

Posit has spent years positioning itself as a polyglot data science company first and foremost.[^2] That matters. If `great-docs` gives R users a familiar on-ramp into Python package documentation, that is a real contribution. Likewise, if it nudges Python users into the broader Quarto ecosystem, even better. I like tools that lower the social cost of crossing language boundaries.

[^2]: Wes McKinney's [JupyterCon talk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wdmf1msbtVs) is still a good snapshot of Posit's broader commitment to Python and multilingual data science.

## Verdict

I am obviously biased here. I like what Posit builds, and I especially like what Quarto makes possible. That said, I am not completely uncritical. I still have not fully moved into Positron as an IDE, mostly because my life involves remote HPC systems, and those environments are great at humbling product claims.

Still: most of what Posit ships is at least thoughtful, and a lot of it is damn near excellent. After reading through the `great-docs` docs rather than just freestyling from the announcement, I think this is a genuinely promising release.

More importantly, I think it opens the door to something I care about more than documentation alone: a Quarto-native, notebook-driven development workflow for Python packages that does not feel bolted on after the fact.

That is where I am headed next.

Part 2 is the proper `great-docs` tour. Part 3 is how I am planning to use it for notebook-driven development, which is where things get fun.
